🩺 NUR221 Pathophysiology - Unit 1 Part 1

Chapters 1, 2 & 4 | Intro, Fluid/Electrolyte/Acid-Base & Pain

📚 Unit 1 Part 1 Overview

📝 Instructor Information

  • Daily Unit Concept Maps: 5-11 conditions covering 3 chapters (Weeks 2, 3, & 4)
  • Submit in Canvas prior to class - save all maps for that part of the unit in 1 document
  • Bring to each class - used as study aide/tool for case studies and tests
  • In Class Group Case Study Activity: 2-4 small groups with different conditions/scenarios
  • 20-25 min brainstorming, 10-15 min/group presenting, information sharing with questions

Unit Structure

This unit covers three foundational chapters in pathophysiology:

📖 Chapter 1: Intro to Pathophysiology

Foundation of disease mechanisms, cellular adaptations, and epidemiology concepts.

💧 Chapter 2: Fluid, Electrolyte & Acid-Base

Body fluid compartments, electrolyte imbalances, and acid-base regulation mechanisms.

⚡ Chapter 4: Pain

Pain mechanisms, types of pain, and the gate control theory of pain perception.

🔑 Key Concepts to Understand

  • Understand anatomy/physiology to make connections to pathophysiology
  • Human Diversity & Pathogenesis impacts: Gender, Age, Race/ethnicity, Socioeconomic status, Locale, Co-morbidities
  • Health-Wellness Continuum concept
  • Key terms are essential building blocks

📖 Chapter 1: Intro to Pathophysiology

Core Definitions

Pathophysiology

Study of the mechanisms of disease; how changes in the cell impact the body's function.

Homeostasis

Tendency towards internal balance.

Disease

Functional impairment of cells, tissues, organs, or organ systems (includes injuries, disorders, illnesses, and syndromes).

Pathogenesis

When the disease process first begins to when the disease presents itself/shows clinical manifestations.

Etiology

Precise cause of disease.

Pathogen

Disease-causing microorganism (can be unknown or known/idiopathic).

Disease Origins & Classifications

Nosocomial Disease

Results from exposure to infection in the healthcare environment; hospital acquired disease.

Iatrogenic Disease

Those that are the inadvertent result of medical treatment.

📝 Teacher's Note: Important Distinction

Nosocomial = Hospital/healthcare acquired (from the environment)

Iatrogenic = Caused by medical treatment (from the treatment itself)

Signs vs Symptoms

Signs (of Disease)

Observable, visible, measurable expression of disease. Can be objectively assessed by healthcare providers.

Symptoms

Reported, subjective manifestations of disease; not always observable or measurable. What the patient tells you.

📝 Teacher's Note: Remember

Signs = What you SEE (objective)

Symptoms = What the patient REPORTS (subjective)

Clinical Manifestations

Systemic

Present throughout the body and not confined to one area. Examples: fever, lethargy, generalized body aching, high blood pressure.

Chronic

Insidious (gradual) onsets and generally last longer than 6 months. Can have periods of remissions (symptom-free) and exacerbations (flaring of symptoms).

Epidemiology Terms

Epidemiology

The study of disease in populations.

Endemic

When the incidence (probability that a disease will occur) and prevalence (number/percentage living with the disease) are predictable and stable.

Epidemic

Dramatic increase in disease incidence in a population.

Pandemic

When a disease epidemic spreads across continents.

Levels of Prevention

Level Focus Example
Primary Prevention Protecting people from developing a disease or injury Vaccinations, seat belts, health education
Secondary Prevention Early detection through screening and early treatment Mammograms, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening
Tertiary Prevention Rehabilitation after diagnosis; preventing complications/progression Cardiac rehab, physical therapy, disease management programs

Diagnostic & Outcome Terms

Diagnosis

Identification of a specific disease through evaluation of signs and symptoms, laboratory tests, or other tools.

Morbidity

Disease rates within a group; sometimes used to indicate functional impairment that certain conditions cause within a population.

Mortality

Relative number of deaths resulting from a particular disease.

Cell Death

Apoptosis

Programmed cell death, a normal occurrence in the body. May increase when cell development is abnormal, cell numbers are excessive, or cells are injured or aged.

Necrosis

Death of one or more cells or a portion of tissue or organ as a result of irreversible damage and NOT a programmed cellular event.

📝 Teacher's Note: Key Distinction

Apoptosis = Programmed, controlled, "planned" cell death (normal)

Necrosis = Unplanned, uncontrolled cell death from injury (pathological)

Cellular Adaptations

Cellular adaptations in response to stress or injury

Atrophy

Decrease in cell size. Example: muscle wasting from disuse.

Hypertrophy

Increase in cell size. Example: cardiac muscle in hypertension.

Hyperplasia

Increase in cell number. Example: prostate gland enlargement.

Metaplasia

Replacement of one mature cell type by another. Example: smokers' airway epithelium.

Dysplasia

Abnormal cell changes; may be precancerous. Example: cervical dysplasia.

💧 Chapter 2: Fluid, Electrolyte & Acid-Base Imbalances

Electrolyte Fundamentals

Electrolytes

Chemicals that produce ions (electrically charged particles) when they dissolve in water.

Cations

Ions with a positive charge: sodium (Na+), calcium (Ca+), hydrogen (H+), and potassium (K+).

Anions

Negatively charged ions: chloride (Cl−), bicarbonate (HCO3−), sulfate (SO4²−), and phosphate (PO4³−).

Transport Mechanisms

Cation Exchange

Transport of one positively charged ion for another in opposite directions across the plasma membrane.

Anion Exchange

Transport of one negatively charged ion for another in opposite directions across the plasma membrane.

Concentration Gradient

Particles or solutes move from a highly concentrated area to a less concentrated area. Aided by transport proteins on the plasma membrane.

📝 Transport Mechanisms (from slides)

  • A) Osmosis: Form of diffusion - Water moves in and out of the kidney (via nephrons) to help balance salt and pH levels
  • B) Facilitated Diffusion: Transport of glucose and amino acids from the bloodstream into the cell
  • C) Active Transport: Transportation of sodium out of the cell and potassium into the cell by the sodium-potassium ATPase pump
  • D) Diffusion: Urea (waste product) excreted by liver

Key Terms

Tonicity

The osmotic pressure or tension of an extracellular solution.

Extracellular Matrix

Combination of collagen structures, excreted proteins, glycoproteins, and various items OUTSIDE of the cell. Helps cells attach to and communicate with nearby cells; important in cell growth, movement, and tissue repair.

Intracellular

Anything INSIDE the cell such as the cytoplasm, Golgi bodies, ribosomes, nucleus, etc.

Plasma

The fluid part of blood.

Fluid Composition

📝 Teacher's Notes - Body Fluid Distribution

  • Body composition = 60% fluid
  • 40% - Intracellular compartment: Fluid inside cell
  • 20% - Extracellular compartment: Fluid outside cell
    • 14% - Interstitial fluid (between cells)
    • 5% - Plasma/intravascular compartment
    • 1% - Transcellular compartment ("3rd space") - spinal cord, peritoneal, pleural, pericardial, and joint spaces (no fluid exchange)
  • Important: 1 L of water weighs 1 kg or 2.2 lb

Body fluid distribution between compartments

Fluid Balance & Regulation

📝 Teacher's Notes - Volume Regulation

  • Volume regulation via renal system
  • Direct → extracellular compartment
  • Indirect → intracellular compartment
  • Fluid movement stimulated by concentration gradient
  • Osmosis: Area of lower concentration of particles (more water) to area of higher concentration of particles (less water)

Fluid Regulation - Intake

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes

Thirst Mechanism:

  • Osmoreceptors (sensory neurons) in the hypothalamic thirst center activated in response to:
    • Cellular dehydration from increased extracellular osmolality
    • Decrease in blood volume
  • Stretch receptors (arterial baroceptors) sense changes in arterial blood pressure (short term)

RAAS (Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System):

  • Angiotensin II indirectly results in thirst sensations related to low blood volume and low blood pressure
  • Addresses both acute and chronic alterations

Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH) / Vasopressin:

  • Regulates fluid volume by controlling excretion of total body water
  • Works at the kidney to promote water reabsorption via aquaporins (special channels in plasma membrane)
  • Moves water from renal collecting ducts into vasculature
  • Reduces fluid loss through decreased urine output

Fluid Regulation - Hormones & Excretion

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes

Aldosterone:

  • Determines the reabsorption of both sodium ions and water from kidney tubules
  • Hormones conserve more fluid when there is a fluid deficit

ANP + BNP (Atrial/B-type Natriuretic Peptides):

  • Released by cardiac muscle fibers in response to increased pressure within cardiac chambers
  • Stimulate elimination of water and sodium in urine to prevent salt-induced hypertension

Diuretics (increase urination):

  • Loop diuretics: Reduce reabsorption of Na+ in thick ascending limb, causing decreased osmolality in interstitial fluid of collecting ducts, impairing ability to concentrate urine
  • Thiazide diuretics: Prevent NaCl reabsorption in distal convoluted tubule; coupled with increased K+ loss in urine and uric acid retention
  • K+-sparing diuretics: Reduce sodium reabsorption in distal tubule and collecting duct; effects of aldosterone inhibited; promotes Na+ and water excretion with increased K+ reabsorption to prevent excessive loss

Altered Fluid Balance

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes on Fluid Imbalances

Hypovolemia:

  • Cause: Excessive body fluid loss, reduction of fluid intake, or loss of fluid to a third space
  • Blood circulation and transport of oxygen and nutrients impaired
  • Decreased blood flow to kidneys activates RAAS → increased sodium and water reabsorption

Hemorrhage:

  • Excessive bleeding, may result in hypovolemia
  • Isotonic fluid loss (H2O + Na+ equally lost)
  • Treatment: Lactated ringers, blood transfusion

Dehydration:

  • Decreased extracellular fluid volume or increased sodium content in relation to water content (hypotonic)
  • Cell shrinks related to fluid moving from inside to outside
  • Treatment: Decrease Na+ and increase H2O

Water Intoxication:

  • Hypotonic hypovolemia; decreased Na+
  • Cell swells related to water moving from outside to inside cell
  • Treatment: Limit H2O and increase Na+; diuretics

Hypervolemia:

  • Excess sodium and water retained in similar proportions; increased volume is isotonic
  • Treatment: Fluid removal (depends on cause)

Edema

📝 Teacher's Notes - Causes of Edema

Increases in the extracellular compartment of the interstitial fluid:

  • Increased capillary filtration pressure: H2O forced into interstitial space
  • Decreased capillary osmotic pressure: Fluid moves into interstitial space across gradient
  • Increased capillary permeability: Proteins leak into interstitial space due to altered capillary wall
  • Obstructed lymph flow: Interstitial fluid not returned to circulation (lymphedema)

Electrolyte Concentrations

Electrolyte distribution between intracellular and extracellular compartments

📝 Teacher's Notes - Electrolyte Distribution

Extracellular:

  • High in sodium, chloride, and calcium
  • Low in potassium, magnesium, and phosphate
  • Moderate levels of bicarbonate

Intracellular:

  • Low levels of calcium
  • Small amounts of sodium, bicarbonate, and chloride
  • Moderate amounts of phosphate and magnesium
  • Potassium is found in greatest concentrations

Sodium (Na+)

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes

  • Most abundant cation in the extracellular compartment
  • Transported out of the cell against its concentration gradient via Na+/K+ ATPase pump
  • 3 K+ out of the cell and 2 Na+ into the cell per each ATP molecule
  • Excreted mainly by kidneys
Imbalance Value Causes Signs/Symptoms
Hyponatremia <135 mEq/L Vomiting, diarrhea, sweating Muscle twitching/weakness (osmotic swelling); hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria/anuria; nausea, vomiting, lethargy, confusion, seizures, coma
Hypernatremia >145 mEq/L Excessive dietary sodium, loss of body water Agitation, restlessness, decreased LOC; thirst, hypertension, tachycardia, edema, weight gain

Potassium (K+)

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes

  • Most abundant intracellular cation
  • Most found in muscle (total body K+ influenced by body size + muscle mass)
Imbalance Value Causes Signs/Symptoms
Hypokalemia <3.5 mEq/L Diuretic use, severe vomiting, diarrhea Dizziness, hypotension, arrhythmias, muscle weakness, leg cramps; nausea, anorexia, abdominal distention
Hyperkalemia >5 mEq/L Inappropriate drug use, renal failure (inadequate excretion) Cardiac arrest, abdominal cramping, flaccid paralysis
⚠️
Critical: Hyperkalemia can cause cardiac arrest - this is a medical emergency!

Chloride (Cl-)

📝 Teacher's Notes

  • Major extracellular anion
  • Often associated with Na+ (important for acid-base balance regulation)
Imbalance Value Causes Signs/Symptoms
Hypochloremia <98 mEq/L Vomiting, diarrhea, diuretics Associated with hyponatremia, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis; excessive tone/tetany, weakness, twitching, shallow/depressed breathing, paralysis, mental confusion
Hyperchloremia >108 mEq/L Severe dehydration, kidney failure, hemodialysis, TBI Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, deep rapid breathing, weakness, headache, diminished cognitive ability, cardiac arrest

Calcium (Ca+)

📝 Teacher's Notes

  • Mostly in bones and teeth; small fraction in extracellular fluid
  • Essential for activity of enzyme systems, generation of action potentials, and muscle contraction
Imbalance Value Causes Signs/Symptoms
Hypocalcemia <8.5 mg/dL Medications (heparin, glucagon), thyroid disorders, severe burns, kidney failure, vitamin D deficiency, sepsis Anxiety, irritability, muscle twitching/cramps/spasms, tetany, laryngospasm, seizure; hypotension, cardiac arrhythmia
Hypercalcemia >10.5 mg/dL Excessive bone breakdown, overactive parathyroid glands, excessive calcium + vitamin D supplements, calcium-containing antacids S/Sx depend on affected body system; can be asymptomatic

🏥 Clinical Tip: Chvostek's & Trousseau's Signs

These are classic signs of hypocalcemia:

  • Chvostek's sign: Facial muscle twitching when facial nerve is tapped
  • Trousseau's sign: Carpal spasm when blood pressure cuff is inflated

Acid-Base Balance

Acid-Base Balance

Homeostatic regulation of pH in the body.

Acids

Substances that donate hydrogen ions.

Bases

Alkaline; substances that accept hydrogen ions.

Anion Gap

Measurement of acid-base balance. Normal range: 10-14 mEq/L (14-18 mEq/L if K+ included). Formula: Na+ − (Cl− + HCO3−)

Compensated vs Uncompensated Acid-Base States

📝 Understanding Compensation

Compensation: The body's attempt to maintain normal pH when one system (respiratory or metabolic) is dysfunctional.

Decompensation: Occurs when kidneys and lungs cannot compensate adequately; ratio changes, serum pH moves out of normal range, affecting cell metabolism and function.

Status pH What It Means
Uncompensated Outside normal range (not 7.35-7.45) Body has not yet tried to correct; both systems show primary imbalance
Partially Compensated Still abnormal but moving toward normal Compensatory system has started to correct but hasn't fully normalized pH
Fully Compensated Within normal range (7.35-7.45) Compensatory system has corrected pH; note: underlying problem still exists

🏥 How to Determine Compensation Status

  1. Check pH: Is it acidotic (<7.35), alkalotic (>7.45), or normal?
  2. Check primary system: Which value (PaCO2 or HCO3-) matches the pH direction?
  3. Check the other system: Is it trying to compensate (moving opposite to correct)?
  4. If pH is normal with abnormal values = Fully Compensated
  5. If pH is abnormal but compensating system is also abnormal = Partially Compensated
  6. If pH is abnormal and only primary system is abnormal = Uncompensated

Buffer Systems

📝 Teacher's Detailed Notes on Buffer Systems

Buffer systems = Mixing of acid and base to resist pH change

Plasma Buffer:

  • Reacts within seconds in response to hydrogen ion concentration

Bicarbonate Buffer:

  • Primary extracellular buffer

Protein Buffer:

  • Responds to moment-to-moment changes to retain pH

H+/K+ Cation Exchange System:

  • Entry of H+ prompts the exit of K+ and vice versa

Respiratory Buffer:

  • Reacts within minutes to excrete CO2 through change in respiratory rate
  • Compensates for alterations but cannot completely restore homeostatic pH
  • Allows extra time for renal buffer system to activate

Renal Buffer:

  • Reacts within hours to days through production, absorption, and excretion of acids, bases, and ions
  • Kidneys are primary regulators of acid-base balance
  • Via: Hydrogen Ion Elimination/Bicarbonate Conservation; Tubular Buffer Systems; Potassium-Hydrogen Exchange; Chloride-Bicarbonate Exchange

Acid-Base Imbalances

Respiratory and metabolic acid-base imbalances

📝 Teacher's Notes - Imbalance Overview

  • Imbalances can be acute or chronic
  • 2 conditions can occur simultaneously
  • Compensation (cause = pH change):
    • Respiratory → initial change in CO2
    • Metabolic → initial change in HCO3-
  • Treatment: Depends on cause; immediate corrective measures → fluid & electrolyte replacement or removal

⚡ Chapter 4: Pain

Pain Fundamentals

Nociceptors

Free sensory nerve endings that are present in most tissues of the body.

Pain Threshold

Refers to the level of stimulation required to activate the nerve ending sufficiently for the individual to perceive pain.

Pain Tolerance

The ability to withstand pain or the perception of its intensity; varies considerably with past pain experience and overall state of health.

Reticular Activating System (RAS)

The brain's awareness of the incoming pain stimuli.

Types of Pain

Central Pain

Caused by dysfunction or damage to the brain or spinal cord. Can be localized or involve a large area. Described as persistent and irritating.

Neuropathic Pain

Caused by trauma or disease involving the peripheral nerves. Described as tingling to a burning or severe shooting pain.

Ischemic Pain

Results from profound, sudden loss of blood flow to an organ or tissues. Decreased blood supply causes hypoxia, leading to tissue damage and release of inflammatory and pain-producing substances. Described as aching, burning, prickling, or strong shooting pain (often in an extremity).

Gate Control Theory

📝 Teacher's Notes - Gate Control Theory

  • "Gates" control pain sensation from peripheral nerves → brain
  • Open gate = Pain felt
  • Closed gate = Pain not felt and/or sensation diminished
  • Non-painful input can "close the gate" to painful input, preventing pain sensation

🔑 Key Concepts

  • Pain = defense mechanism
  • Results from stimulation of nociceptors by ischemia, chemical mediators, or distention of tissue
  • Classification of Pain by: Quality, Duration, Location

Case Study Example (from textbook)

📋 Case: Acute Pain (Textbook pg 62)

Scenario

L.Y. is a healthy 13-year-old teenager who had all her wisdom teeth removed 6 hours ago and is experiencing significant pain. She has been prescribed acetaminophen and codeine for pain relief and is at home recovering. Her mother wants her to rest and stop text-messaging her friends about her dental surgery.

🧠 Thinking Questions

1
How do acetaminophen and codeine act to reduce pain? What is a side effect of high levels of each drug? Why has the dentist prescribed only a limited supply?
2
How does L.Y.'s text-messaging behavior affect her perception of pain?
3
Does L.Y. need to rest in bed quietly to reduce pain?
4
L.Y. becomes increasingly irritated with her mother and tells her to "get off my case." How does L.Y.'s stress affect her pain perception?

💼 Clinical Case Studies

📋 Case 1: Postoperative Respiratory Complications

Scenario

A nurse is caring for a client who is postoperative and whose respirations are shallow and 9/min. Which acid-base imbalance should the nurse identify the client as being at risk for developing initially?

🧠 Clinical Thinking Process

1
Identify the problem: Shallow, slow respirations (9/min is bradypnea)
2
Connect to physiology: Slow/shallow breathing = CO2 retention
3
Apply acid-base knowledge: CO2 is acidic; increased CO2 = acidosis
4
Determine type: Respiratory cause = Respiratory acidosis
Answer: Respiratory Acidosis - Slow, shallow breathing causes CO2 retention. CO2 combines with water to form carbonic acid, decreasing pH. The respiratory system is the cause, so it's respiratory acidosis.

📋 Case 2: Metabolic Acidosis Risk

Scenario

A nurse is reviewing medical records of four clients with acid-base imbalance. Which client is at risk for metabolic acidosis?

  • 1) A client who has diarrhea
  • 2) A client who is vomiting
  • 3) A client taking a thiazide diuretic
  • 4) A client who has salicylate intoxication
Answer: 1) A client who has diarrhea - Diarrhea causes loss of bicarbonate-rich intestinal fluids. Bicarbonate is a base; losing it causes acidosis. Since it's a metabolic (GI) loss, it's metabolic acidosis. Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis (loss of acid). Thiazides can cause alkalosis. Salicylate intoxication can cause mixed acid-base disorders.

📋 Case 3: Electrolyte Imbalance from NG Tube

Scenario

A nurse is caring for a client who has a postoperative ileus and an NG tube that has drained 2,500 mL in the past 6 hours. Which electrolyte imbalance should the nurse monitor for?

  • 1) Elevated sodium level
  • 2) Decreased potassium level
  • 3) Elevated magnesium level
  • 4) Decreased calcium level
Answer: 2) Decreased potassium level - Gastric fluid contains significant potassium. Large-volume NG drainage causes hypokalemia. This can lead to cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, and other complications. Monitor K+ levels closely.

📋 Case 4: Dehydration Assessment

Scenario

A nurse in a community clinic is assessing an older adult client for manifestations of dehydration. Which finding should the nurse expect?

  • 1) Hypothermia
  • 2) Protruding eyeballs
  • 3) Elevated blood pressure
  • 4) Furrows in the tongue
Answer: 4) Furrows in the tongue - Dehydration causes dry mucous membranes. The tongue may appear dry with longitudinal furrows. Other signs in older adults: tachycardia, hypotension, confusion, poor skin turgor. Note: Older adults may not show typical signs due to aging changes.
